stories.headline: TRUMP EXTENDS IRAN CEASEFIRE AS PEACE TALKS STALL; NAVAL BLOCKADE REMAINS
stories.body: President Trump extended the U.S.-Iran ceasefire indefinitely Tuesday night, citing a 'seriously fractured' Iranian government, while maintaining the naval blockade of Iranian ports now in its second week. Iran has refused to negotiate under the 'shadow of threats,' and VP Vance's trip to Pakistan for a second round of talks was canceled. Brent briefly hit $98.48 before easing — oil is up 30%+ since the Feb. 28 attack.

stories.headline: OIL PULLS BACK FROM $98 PEAK BUT STAYS ELEVATED ON STRAIT OF HORMUZ RISK
stories.body: WTI fell 0.38% to $89.33 and Brent eased from its $98.48 high after the ceasefire extension calmed the most acute fears. Prices remain ~30% above pre-war levels as the blockade keeps Persian Gulf supply in question. Gold hit $4,753, +0.68%, as safe-haven demand persists alongside equity recovery.

stories.headline: MARCH CPI SURGES TO 3.3% YOY AS IRAN WAR ENERGY SPIKE HITS CONSUMER PRICES
stories.body: U.S. CPI rose 0.9% MoM and 3.3% YoY in March, a sharp jump from 2.4% in February, as energy costs spiked in the weeks following the Feb. 28 U.S.-Israel strike on Iran. The print complicates the Fed's rate path — inflation is accelerating but from a geopolitical supply shock, not excess demand, limiting how much rate policy can help.

stories.headline: STRONG Q1 RESULTS BUT COMPANIES HOLD GUIDANCE; GE AEROSPACE EPITOMIZES CAUTION
stories.body: Q1 earnings have broadly beaten estimates, but corporate guidance remains conservative. GE Aerospace CEO Larry Culp told CNBC the company would have raised full-year guidance 'if not for current events,' opting to hold forecasts flat. The pattern is widespread — strong backward-looking results, muted forward outlooks tied to geopolitical uncertainty and oil-price volatility.

stories.headline: EQUITIES REBOUND WEDNESDAY; S&P +0.76% TO 7,118 AFTER TUESDAY'S IRAN-DRIVEN DROP
stories.body: All four major indices recovered Wednesday — S&P 500 +0.76%, Nasdaq +0.93%, Dow +0.83%, Russell 2000 +1.05% — after Trump's ceasefire extension eased peak geopolitical fears. Tuesday the S&P had fallen 0.63% to 7,064 on fears a deal would collapse. The DXY held near 98.35, edging down 0.04% as safe-haven dollar demand softened slightly.

stories.headline: MICHIGAN WINS 2026 NCAA TITLE; TRANSFER PORTAL NOW THE SPORT'S MAIN STAGE
stories.body: Michigan claimed the 2026 national championship on April 6, defeating UConn 69-63 in Indianapolis. With the season complete, the transfer portal is now where rosters are being built — blue bloods are reshaping lineups and Washington added Davidson sharpshooter Parker Friedrichsen as one of the portal's early movers.

stories.headline: WEMBANYAMA EXITS WITH CONCUSSION; BLAZERS EVEN SERIES, 76ERS STUN CELTICS IN GAME 2s
stories.body: Victor Wembanyama suffered a concussion in the second quarter of Spurs-Trail Blazers Game 2, with Scoot Henderson (31 pts, 5 3PM) powering Portland to a 106-103 win to tie the series 1-1. In Boston, VJ Edgecombe (30 pts, 10 reb) and Tyrese Maxey (29 pts) led the 76ers to a 111-97 upset, also tying their series. Wembanyama's availability for Game 3 is uncertain.

stories.headline: 2026 NFL DRAFT OPENS THURSDAY IN PITTSBURGH; RAIDERS PICK FIRST, RODGERS STATUS STILL UNKNOWN
stories.body: The NFL Draft begins Thursday in Pittsburgh (R1 April 23, R2-3 April 24, R4-7 April 25). The Las Vegas Raiders hold the #1 pick, expected to go to Fernando Mendoza. Aaron Rodgers has still not told the Steelers whether he'll play in 2026 — the team opens minicamp without an answer. First-round pick clocks are now 8 minutes, down from 10.

stories.headline: LANE KIFFIN AT LSU LEADS SPRING HEADLINES; CONTROVERSIAL SPECIAL TEAMS RULE PASSES DESPITE COACH OPPOSITION
stories.body: Lane Kiffin's move from Ole Miss to LSU — announced before Ole Miss's playoff run concluded — makes him the marquee coaching storyline of spring 2026. A new special teams rule drew near-unanimous opposition from coaches at an NCAA briefing, yet passed anyway. Spring games are returning at 40+ Power Four programs including Auburn, Nebraska, Oklahoma, and Texas.

sports.detail: Lost Game 1 vs. Magic 101-112 (Cade Cunningham 39 pts). Game 2 tonight 7:10 PM ET on ESPN — Magic lead series 1-0.
Lost to Oakland A's. Cal Raleigh HR, avg .177. 6 losses in last 8. A's Langeliers hit 417-ft blast for 7th straight road win.
Super Bowl champs hold pick #32 (R1). GM Schneider open to trading down. Draft begins Thursday in Pittsburgh on ESPN/ABC.
Top 2027 recruits visiting Montlake this week. Season opener vs. Indiana (defending national champs) set for Nov. 21.
Signed Davidson transfer Parker Friedrichsen (sharpshooter, 33 starts this season). Missed out on #1 recruit Tyran Stokes (Oregon/Kentucky/Kansas final 3).

watchlist: NFL Draft Round 1 — Thursday April 23, Pittsburgh, ESPN/ABC. Raiders pick #1 (Mendoza). Seahawks at #32.
Iran ceasefire negotiations ongoing — blockade remains, talks stalled. Any escalation = oil spike toward $100.
Fed rate path: March CPI at 3.3% YoY limits cut room. Watch May FOMC statement for updated guidance.
Pistons Game 2 tonight vs. Magic — 7:10 PM ET, ESPN. Pistons must respond after 112-101 Game 1 loss.
Mariners homestand continues vs. A's — Seattle has lost 6 of last 8 and needs to reverse momentum.

deepDive.title: Strait of Hormuz: How a 21-Mile Chokepoint Becomes a Global Inflation Tax
content: The U.S.-Iran ceasefire extension kept oil prices elevated Wednesday — Brent briefly touched $98.48 before easing — and the mechanism behind that move is the Strait of Hormuz, the world's most critical energy chokepoint. The Strait is just 21 miles wide at its narrowest navigable point and handles roughly 17-20 million barrels per day, approximately 20% of global petroleum liquids trade. When military conflict or a blockade threatens passage, the risk premium embedded in crude oil prices spikes immediately — not because oil has stopped flowing, but because traders price the probability of a larger disruption into every barrel.

The core mechanism: oil is a globally priced commodity, so any credible supply threat raises the clearing price of the marginal barrel. Futures traders buy forward contracts to hedge against disruption, driving spot prices higher. Simultaneously, shippers face war-risk insurance surcharges for transiting the Persian Gulf — those premiums add directly to transport costs and widen the Brent-WTI spread, since Brent pricing is more exposed to Middle East supply routes. Since the Feb. 28 attack, Brent is up ~30% without a sustained drop in actual barrels delivered — that is pure risk premium embedded in every settlement price.

What most people miss: energy shocks transmit into headline CPI in a predictable 60-90 day cascade. Gasoline moves within days, then jet fuel and diesel, then manufacturing and trucking costs, then durable goods pricing. March's 0.9% MoM CPI print lands almost exactly on schedule from the Feb. 28 attack. The 1973 Arab embargo is the textbook precedent: OPEC's production cut drove U.S. CPI from 6.2% to 12.3% within a year. More instructively, once energy embeds in services pricing and wage negotiations, disinflation is structurally slower even after the shock fades — freight contracts and union agreements reset to a higher baseline and take years to unwind.

For the current market setup, this creates a genuine Fed policy trap. Inflation is accelerating, but from a geopolitical supply shock rather than excess demand, meaning rate hikes cannot fix it — you cannot raise rates into cheaper oil. Cutting rates to ease growth concerns risks entrenching energy-driven inflation expectations. The 10-year yield at 4.30% reflects markets pricing 'higher for longer' with no clean exit. The resolution depends almost entirely on whether Iran submits a ceasefire proposal in Islamabad — not on anything Powell announces at the May FOMC. Watch the Strait, not the dot plot.
